title: Authentication is not authorization
description: Authentication proves who a principal is; authorization decides what that principal may do — a valid session says nothing about access to a specific resource.
body: Use when reasoning about access bugs or designing a protected endpoint. Authentication establishes identity (a valid token/session); authorization is the per-action, per-resource permission decision that must run after identity is known. Conflating them causes IDOR and privilege-escalation bugs where any logged-in user reaches another's data. Do not treat 'has a valid session' as 'may perform this action'. Actions: separate the two layers, re-check authorization on every sensitive operation, and centralize the permission model. Validation signals include tests proving an authenticated-but-unauthorized request is denied. Risk boundary: a feature that only authenticates and never authorizes per-resource must be escalated as an access-control gap.
